title: Gene modeling and prediction
description: {"blocks":[{"key":"9dbdn","text":"We performed a considerate prediction of intact protein-coding gene models using three independent approaches, i.e.,de novoprediction, homology-based method, and RNA-Seq approach.","type":"unstyled","depth":0,"inlineStyleRanges":[],"entityRanges":[],"data":[]}],"entityMap":{}}
steps: The repeat masked assembly was firstly annotated by AUGUSTUS (version 3.3) with default parameters, which was a de novopredictor based on the self-trained model. AUGUSTUS 3.3 Linux Centos 6

In the homology-based prediction, we used seven species as reference datasets,i.e., Elaeis guineensis, Phoenix dactylifera , Brachypodium distachyon, Oryza sativa, Setaria italic, Sorghum bicolor, and Zea mays (see Availability of supporting data for individual genome version). Their protein sequences were downloaded for ENSEMBL database [30]and were aligned to the C. simplicifolius and D. jenkinsiana assembly using TBLASTN (version 2.2.26) with an E-value cutoff of 1e-5, respectively. Then, the splicing patterns were generated by GeneWise (version 2.0). TBLASTN 2.2.26 Linux Centos 6 GeneWise 2.0 Linux Centos 6

In the RNA-Seq analysis, HISAT2 (version 2.0.2) was used to identify exon-intron splicing junctions and refine the alignment of the RNA-Seq reads to the genome. Then, we used Cufflinks (version 2.2.1) to define some protein-coding gene models in C. simplicifoliusand D. jenkinsiana, respectively HISAT 2 Linux Centos 6 Cufflinks 2.2.1 Linux Centos 6
We integrated the evidences from the three above independent predictions using MARKER (version 2). MAKER 2 Linux Centos 6
creator: Hansheng Zhao
